But related to, like, what is possible in our newer model, I think, luckily, some of that complexity of narrowing the attention of the editing team into a more focused time and, you know, maybe not necessarily less accounts than they had before.
But we're asking them to do dual roles today, where essentially they are both playing that editing role and leading.

The discussion, that's not true of every account, but a lot of our accounts, that's how that has worked, should hopefully enable us to find more time to start to put time towards how do you start to address some of these different complexities.
And then if you are hitting points of friction and are blocked, what are your resources available and who do you go to to pull them in in a more meaningful way?
Today, how that works is largely you may pull in someone like myself, a Panzer, whoever it may be.
At times, it probably depends on the person who they go to.
It's probably not as clear as it should be for them.
Then they have a path to go to the client engineering team and scope a ticket for them.
I think where we're going now should make it a bit more clear of when you face this, go to that team.
And this is the process of actually doing that and engaging them.
There's certainly more to define of like what kicks that off, how.
How you do that?
How do we get that in front of the team so they understand it?
But I think, like, directionally, we're getting to a place where that should be easier.
So I feel like we're doing a lot of the right things.
I think we're going to have to probably have some hard discussions and choices of, like, should we continue to operate with some of these customers today?
Like, it can be painful to let some of them go and just, but some of that churn can be helpful for us because it's, we can't get too stuck on the logo.

And we may be missing the forest or the trees of, like, what actually are the compounding effects of us, like, keeping an account like that and not building more repeatable leverage across the company.
Can you give me two examples of customers that might be good candidates to part ways with?
Yeah, the one that is closest to me, I would say, is Okta.
Amazing brand.
We're working
With the Okta and Nos Zero team, I really personally like working with those folks and, you know, great logos to have for us.
But what we are doing for them is in no way transferable to any of our other accounts.
Theoretically, it could open up some paths, guess, of like, do we make this an offering in the future?
But nothing that has really, you know, come forward that that's true yet.
But essentially, what are you doing for them?
Yeah, of course.

Essentially, we are working with someone who is kind of like working in this kind of unique floating role at Okta and Nos Zero where isn't necessarily like solid line into marketing, isn't solid line into sales, but is trying to help them figure out, you know, meaty challenges, almost in an advising type role to, okay, like, here's how we can build repeatable systems for you all.
To address some of these keys.
And so one of those things were, you know, their SDR team really not understanding the business at a deep enough level, the accounts they were trying to break into at a deep enough level.

And so what we built with them is this system of essentially taking different signals that they have, ingesting that, creating briefs and playbooks for these SDRs, and then trying to think about how do we get these briefs in the hands of those folks?
Like, what does that look like?
You know, we've tried to do different things of getting into systems like Gemini or Notebook LM and, like, how they can maybe extract further information from this.
But this is one of those things where it's just continued to grow layers.
It's shifted directions.
Their clarity of what they actually need from us is quite difficult at times.
I actually have a level-setting conversation with them on Monday where it's just kind of, I know, I scheduled a longer meeting.
It's kind of like a little bit of, like...
Let's put a stake in the ground and actually more clearly establish where do we want to be in a month or two or whatever.
I've had some recent conversations with them on focus and, you know, was teasing the idea of like maybe we're not a fit for each other in the not too distant future.
That led to us kind of narrowing, but now it's kind of creeping again out, right?
And so like I think that's just the reality of how this would go.
And also it pulls me in like, you know, I shouldn't be spending my time on this like when I can be doing all these other things.
And so it's a hard thing to pass off.
How much do they pay to secure it?
I'm trying to remember.
I think it is 12K a month.
I can look it up too.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I feel like we pitched them at 15 and they got us down to 12.
It's only a six month deal too.
And so it's like a shorter deal.
It's not our lowest paying, but, you know, so it's something we have, you know, also explored.
Is there a way of like getting in?
With the team there, that would be a better fit for us in terms of like operationally.
Like, you know, what do you need in terms of content that aligns more to a typical rhythm?
Yeah.
We have maybe some opportunity there, but yeah, I think it's, that's a good example.
A couple others we have is Surge and AugmentCode.
Those two pull in JSON quite a bit.
And both of them are kind of just different in their own eyes.
But I mean, Surge is, and you maybe have already heard this, like by far our highest paying customer.
Yeah.
Outrageous what they're paying to the average.
And that's just taken all sorts of twists and turns of like all the different things we've done for them.
Like, I don't think what we have in the scope of our contract today is anywhere close to a reflection of like the work we're actually doing.
And sometimes that's the reality of like when someone's paying you that much, it's kind of like, you know, figured out.
But then at the same time, like that, that is a hard thing for a JSON to pass.
So.
So.
Thank
Because of that.
Augment code, also quite high paying, a little bit closer to what is in their contract we're executing on, but still very like one of those things that's just custom and hard to pass off.
And so I believe we have a custom kind of like focus team as a part of this rollout.

But, you know, I think we have more to define of like, how does like the work strategically work for those accounts that are in the delivery org, whether it's an augment or someone else in the future.
We haven't really like clearly established what, like, how do we get Jason out of that?
How does my team get involved in that?
You know, Serge almost sits separate from that, but like, you know, like a web flow, an augment.
Those are some that are, I believe, living in that org that I don't know if we fully nailed, like, how does strategy work for these moving forward?
How does strategic support rather work for these?
Okay.
I'm just taking quick notes about like sort of my takeaways, but before I finish that, when you're thinking about what you're about to head up, the shared strategy team, right?
Tell me about how are you going to measure success about whether this is working or not?
What are you looking for?
Still working to refine that a bit.
we'd love your input.
Bridget has given me some helpful feedback as well.

think directionally, some of the things around, you know, thinking about like a percentage of tickets completed in sprints, weighing some level of complexity, ensuring that we have clear clarity over some of that client health metrics this team can influence and own.
And are they positively moving those metrics such as like performance, for example, if you're putting together a strategy that doesn't lead to performance?
And the team effectively executed on that to the scope we established, and that's on us, right?
Right.
Or maybe factors out of our control, but that's how we should be thinking about it.
And then also building playbooks.
And so things that can be, you know, hopefully continuing to build leverage in the team.

So there's certain things maybe we don't always have to be pulled in for, but also can be establishing the standard for other directors and the strategy team to operate under the same, like, oh, we've done this before, and here's how we approach it.
And so we should be able to do this more efficiently as a result.

The additional thing that I don't have a measurement to this, because I'm really thinking about this as like a kind of strategy and insights team, because I think a thing we have really not done a good enough job at is how do we extract and share insights that we're learning across our accounts, both internally and externally consistently.
And so what are key trends that we're learning?
What are things we've tested that have given us rich learnings?
How do we package that up and give that to the team internally so they can learn from that and be inspired by that?
And it just creates that culture of wanting to share in that way.

think we really need to establish more of that within our culture, but also then can feed into the engine with a JSON and a Panzer of, as we're doing go-to-market things, some of our competitors are way more on top of that today than we are.
Like, that is, you know, when we do our workshops and webinars, like, we're able to share that off, but we have to build that engine in a variety of ways.
That's not always, like, largely how that works today is we pull in Marcel, we figure out what we're going to present.
He does his heroics, and we can't rely on his heroics for that stuff.
So, you know, that is a key piece that I'm still thinking through how particularly that works and can relate to the playbooks probably to some degree.
But to me, it's just a gap that I think it makes sense for this team to start.
Okay.

So if I were to summarize when I'm kind of thinking through scaling, and again, if I want to support all of you getting to 100 customers in a way that feels like you're marching without hair on fire, a couple simple things.
One is sort of employee onboarding consistency.
And is there sort of this published engagement roadmap that people know what are the handoffs?
How do you set expectations on a call?
When do you cancel calls and you like opt for momentum?
And, you know, it sounds like there's some really great discipline outlined, but the operationalization of it might be inconsistent.
And so making that transparent of what is the rhythm of engagement?
What are the assets?
What are the measurements of success?
And making sure that's understood, like from the moment someone's like onboarding.
The second thing I heard you say is having a better and more consistent understanding of A, when to escalate.
And when are you calling an account red versus, you know, so knowing when to escalate for what and to whom sounds like that needs, could use some definition.
Yeah.
And I would say one thing I would add to that, that's on my mind.
And this is probably more a proactive thing that I think we need to assess how often this comes up.
But how do we also build systems that uncover signals that aren't reliant on the engagement managers to report up and flag something?
How also are we setting, like, you know, oh, traffic or whatever metric is waning in this account or something is, like, there should be programmatic signals that we can start to build.
And, like, that gets flagged and we can hold the team accountable and also, like, take some of the weight off them.
And so, you know, where that fits, how we do that, when we do that, I'm not yet sure.
But I have a strong hunch that, like, related to that, we should be thinking about and doing that.
Okay.
And the other one, which is sort of related to all the above, is from a handoff perspective.
saying, I'll saying, I'll I'll I'll
I don't think there is any account that won't drift a little bit as they get in and as they learn, they're going to ask for slightly different things.

But the difference is being very intentional about it and always tying it back to sort of what matters to them versus sort of drifting and then realizing a year later that they're going to churn because there's someone who hasn't been involved.
Just looking at the contract and saying, you know, anything else, like if you, if we were going to meet in three months from now, what would you say, Ella, thank you for doing this?
Like, AKA, what is, what would be sort of a pain point just from your explicit lens, personal lens that you'd want to make sure I'm looking at?
And you would say you were successful because you helped us do this.
Great question.
Honestly, I'm not sure it's so different than what you had mentioned at the top of like establishing that standard of onboarding both custom.
Murders and people and the expectations and how all that, you know, is brought together in a clear to understand and meaningful way.
You know, having those clear places to go, those clear playbooks, those, you know, ways we can hold the team accountable to that, to me, I feel like just helps us in so many places.
Just knowing we've kind of been band-aiding that in so many ways.
I'll think about that a little bit more.
have no doubt there will be other things there.

But to me, like, that just, I just feel like there's so many cascading effects of that not being right, that at times we can get distracted in treating the symptoms of that rather than the root cause that started with that specifically.
Right.
Is my strong hypothesis there.
Yeah, let me noodle.
It's a good way of thinking about it.
You know, I know we have these kind of like SWAT sessions that we're going to be going through.
So I think that's going to be a good, helpful place to like, you know.
Yeah, I'm looking forward to that today.
Yeah, to kind of just hear what happens in there for sure.

If there's one thing that I just randomly kind of came to mind, I didn't share related to some of the questions you were mentioning earlier, like kind of like risk points of, you know, how to think about is the team enabled for this change or not.
As we think about that onboarding, how do we also think about like reboarding, however you want to think about it.
Like people who like haven't gone through that and like are at that standard and like how do we get them to that standard?
Like as one example that comes to mind, I sometimes try to stop myself and understand that I have spent so many years like analyzing and reporting up on data how do get
To like senior folks that it's just kind of like second nature to me of like the best practices and how to think about it.
And I've trained varying folks to that.
But I think we have some engagement managers that are going to need some coaching in that.
And if they are the ones who are having those conversations week to week, like what is that expectation?
How does that work?
Like if some of that can be trainings and playbooks, my team can help build.
But as we think about onboarding folks, that should be a part of that onboarding process.
So again, going back to like, as we reflect in a line on like, what is that standard we want to set?
Like, is our team even at that today?
How do we address it?
And that gets back to sort of what I asked you earlier is let's say we go, it's kind of like easy to define it.
But the question is, could the team do it?

And I think that's, you just gave a really great example for me of what to look for of, yeah, we might outline this, but then there's an inevitable training of, well, what does it actually mean to present success metrics and talk about the why behind it and...

The other thing, and I want to respect your time because I know we're out, but that I've heard is this really interesting friction that I'm kind of fascinated by, just sort of more generally speaking, is what you all do is really hard and maybe not all customers understand how hard it is.
Right.
And so if you get to a contract renewal and they're like, yeah, whatever, I think I can go do it.
That's a problem.
You know, so how do you do a better, how do we also help expose just the complexity that you're solving and the why you remaining?
And then secondly, how do we learn from all of that?
So as you productize it over time, you know, there's like, there's a couple of really cool dimensions.
And then how are you creating mini use, like out in the marketplace or your community and whatever.
There's like many fun things for you guys.
I think that's what's exciting for us.
There is so many loop opportunities here in so many places.
And we need to prioritize the right things.
need to, you know, actually make that, bring that to life.
But that's been a big thing that's been exciting for me from day one of just like that's different about the opportunity we have to the average.
Right.
So, yeah.
Right.
Right.
Okay.
Well, I'll let you go.
Thank you so much for your time.
And talk to you later this afternoon.
But, and I'll also be in touch.
Of course.
I'm happy to have more of these outside of that SWAT session.
And, you know, I'm sure we'll continue to refine different ways that are helpful to bring you in to the fold.
Super.
All right.
Take care.
Talk to you soon.
You as well.
All right.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Deep dive on GrowthX's delivery experience, team challenges, and new org structure.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=73.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Inconsistent Delivery:** Delivery is inconsistent, focusing on "business-as-usual" updates instead of strategic outcomes. This stems from a lack of a standardized customer journey and unclear success metrics.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=762.0)
  - [**Capacity Risk:** The new Engagement Manager (EM) model (7–9 accounts) is untested. The planned shift to biweekly meetings is intended to create capacity, but this must be balanced against the risk of losing customer momentum.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=1156.0)
  - [**Unscalable Accounts:** High-touch, custom accounts (e.g., Okta at $12k/mo, Surge) are a major drain on senior resources, especially Jason, and prevent the development of repeatable processes.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2086.0)
  - [**New Strategy Team:** Kyle will lead a new "shared strategy" team to standardize delivery, build playbooks, and capture/share insights across accounts.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=596.0)

## Topics

### The Problem: Inconsistent Delivery & Unclear Success

  - [Delivery is inconsistent, focusing on "business-as-usual" updates (e.g., content output) instead of strategic outcomes.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=875.0)
  - [The root cause is a lack of a standardized customer journey and unclear success metrics, which leads to:](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=762.0)
      - [**Vague Strategy Sprint Exit Criteria:** No clear definition of what makes an account "ready" for ongoing delivery.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=825.0)
      - [**Fragmented Onboarding:** No consistent, published engagement roadmap for new employees.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=1557.0)
      - [**Unclear Escalation Paths:** Team members are unsure when to escalate or to whom.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=1997.0)
  - [The current weekly meeting cadence often feels like "motion" without "momentum," as teams can struggle to find meaningful discussion points.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=1484.0)

### The Solution: New Org Structure & Strategy Team

  - [The new EM model (7–9 accounts) aims to create capacity by separating client-facing roles from editing duties.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=1954.0)
  - [The planned shift to biweekly meetings is intended to give EMs more time for strategic work, but this must be balanced against the risk of losing customer momentum.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=1208.0)
  - [Kyle's new "shared strategy" team will standardize delivery by:](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2403.0)
      - [**Building Playbooks:** Creating repeatable processes for common challenges.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2473.0)
      - [**Capturing Insights:** Systematizing the sharing of learnings across accounts.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2497.0)
      - [**Measuring Success:** Tracking metrics like ticket completion, client performance, and playbook creation.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2414.0)

### The Challenge: Unscalable Accounts

  - [High-touch, custom accounts are a major drain on senior resources, especially Jason, and prevent the development of repeatable processes.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2086.0)
  - [**Okta ($12k/mo):** A custom project building SDR playbooks. The work is not transferable, and the scope is creeping. Kyle is scheduling a level-setting conversation.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2086.0)
  - [**Surge & AugmentCode:** High-paying but highly custom accounts that rely heavily on Jason.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2296.0)
  - [**Strategic Decision:** GrowthX must decide if the brand value of these logos outweighs the operational cost and drag on scalability.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2054.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Ella:**](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2583.0)
      - [Define a standardized customer journey roadmap with clear handoffs and success criteria.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2583.0)
      - [Formalize escalation paths and define when to flag an account as "red."](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2631.0)
      - [Develop a "re-boarding" plan to upskill existing employees on the new delivery standards.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2833.0)
      - [Investigate programmatic signals to proactively flag account health issues.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2651.0)
  - [**Kyle:**](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2403.0)
      - [Refine success metrics for the new shared strategy team.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2403.0)
      - [Establish a process for capturing and sharing insights across accounts.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2497.0)
      - [Lead the level-setting conversation with Okta to clarify scope.](https://fathom.video/share/xvxxiygRwQczLSycaffG5Qz9KFWPj3zA?tab=summary&timestamp=2206.0)

action_items.description: Confirm Okta contract terms (monthly fee, term)
